text: Hello everybody. Today I am sharing a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal recurrent umbilical hernia repair. This is a middle-aged gentleman that came to me after having a primary umbilical hernia repair for what was reported as a two centimeter hernia two or three years ago somewhere else. The recurrent hernia was only two centimeters again but causing him some pain. So we went ahead and proceeded with this case and when I do these cases I'm going into them hoping that the patient will have favorable enough peritoneum to do this. But knowing that, if necessary, I can bail out to an IPOM plus or a retro-rectus repair if maybe the hernia defect was larger.

The surgeon addresses the recurrent umbilical hernia by removing preperitoneal fat and the hernia sac, encountering thick, scarred tissue indicative of a recurrent condition. The hernia sac is successfully taken down, but excessive traction leads to a full-thickness burn of the umbilical skin, which is excised and the umbilicus is reconstructed.

The surgeon navigates to the contralateral side, carefully dissecting the preperitoneal fat to reach the correct plane for peritoneum removal. Using a technique of 'cut, push, cut, push' with minimal tension, cold dissection is employed to avoid perforations. Once the dissection is complete, the mesh is selected, sutured intra-abdominally, and placed in the pre-peritoneal pocket.

The surgeon closes the defect in two layers using a running non-absorbable number one PDS suture. Despite struggling with the eighteen-inch suture, the surgeon ensures the closure is secure. The surgeon then places a fifteen by fifteen centimeter flat polypropylene uncoated mesh into the pre-peritoneal pocket, performing additional dissection on the inferior aspect to ensure the mesh lays flat.

The surgeon places eight tacking sutures, using two Vicryl sutures and three on each side, with two more centrally to secure the mesh against the fascia. A 3-0 barbed suture is used to close the pocket, opting for a Stratofix due to the unavailability of the preferred V-lock suture.

So I did this case with two eight millimeter ports and one twelve millimeter port, which means I did have to close that fascial defect at the twelve millimeter site, which is always the thing that hurts the patients the most. So trying to get away from using that twelve millimeter port. However, getting into these cases and not knowing for sure that I'm going to be able to do the preperitoneal repair and therefore get the mesh down the eight millimeter trocar, I've still been putting in the twelve in case I need to do an eye palm.
